title: General Anesthesia Induction with Post-Induction Hypotension
summary: A 68-year-old hypertensive man on lisinopril (taken the morning of surgery) develops significant hypotension after a routine propofol induction. Learners must recognize the pressure drop, treat it with vasopressors and fluid, and adjust the anesthetic.
learningObjectives: Identify patient factors that predispose to post-induction hypotension (age, ACE inhibitor use, hypertension, hypovolemia).
Titrate induction agents appropriately in an elderly patient on renin-angiotensin blockade.
Recognize post-induction hypotension promptly using appropriate monitoring frequency.
Select and dose vasopressors (phenylephrine vs. ephedrine) based on heart rate and clinical context.
Apply adjunctive measures: fluid bolus, reduction of volatile agent, and increased blood pressure monitoring frequency.
Generate a differential diagnosis for hypotension after induction beyond drug effect.
setup: Standard OR setup with anesthesia machine, circle circuit, and full ASA monitors applied to the manikin.
20 g IV in the left forearm running lactated Ringer's; label a second bag of crystalloid as available for bolus.
Prepare labeled syringes: propofol, fentanyl, rocuronium, phenylephrine 100 mcg/mL, ephedrine 5 mg/mL.
Set the NIBP cycle to 5 minutes at case start so learners must consider shortening it.
Faculty confederate plays the circulating nurse and can answer questions about the patient chart.
Chart shows lisinopril taken with a sip of water at 06:00 on the day of surgery.

patient.name: Walter Briggs
medications: Lisinopril 20 mg daily (taken morning of surgery)
pmh: NPO since midnight; clear liquids until 04:00

airway.notes: No anticipated airway difficulty; prior uneventful general anesthetic in 2019.

patient.plan: Elective open inguinal hernia repair under general anesthesia with an endotracheal tube. Standard IV induction, sevoflurane maintenance.

phases.label: Pre-induction
phases.description: Room setup, monitors, preoxygenation, IV check.

phases.label: Induction
phases.description: IV induction, airway management, start of volatile agent.

phases.label: Resolution
phases.description: Vitals stabilize and the case proceeds.

events.label: Propofol induction given
events.description: Propofol 1.5-2 mg/kg IV with fentanyl 1-2 mcg/kg. Depth falls to a surgical level, blood pressure drifts down modestly, patient becomes apneic and is mask ventilated.

events.label: Sevoflurane started at 2%
events.description: After intubation, sevoflurane is dialed to 2% inspired; end-tidal climbs toward 1.8% over the next few minutes.

events.label: Post-induction hypotension onset
events.description: Vasodilation from propofol and sevoflurane, unopposed in a chronically ACE-inhibited patient, drops the blood pressure to 72/40. Heart rate compensation is blunted.

events.label: Hypotension worsens (untreated)
events.description: If no treatment is given, MAP continues to fall: 58/32 with early tachycardia and a mild SpO2 dip. Fire if learners fail to act.

events.label: Phenylephrine 100 mcg IV effect
events.description: Phenylephrine 100 mcg IV bolus: SBP rises to the high 90s within about a minute with mild reflex slowing of the heart rate. May be repeated.

events.label: Ephedrine 5-10 mg IV effect
events.description: Ephedrine 5-10 mg IV bolus: mixed direct and indirect sympathomimetic effect raises both blood pressure and heart rate over 1-2 minutes.

events.label: Crystalloid bolus 500 mL effect
events.description: A 500 mL lactated Ringer's bolus improves preload; blood pressure rises gradually over about 4 minutes.

events.label: Sevoflurane reduced to 1%
events.description: Inspired sevoflurane turned down to 1%; end-tidal falls toward 1.2% and vasodilation lessens. Depth lightens toward the upper end of the surgical range.

events.label: Surgical incision
events.description: Skin incision provides sympathetic stimulation; blood pressure and heart rate rise if depth is adequate but hemodynamics were marginal.

events.label: Hemodynamics stabilize
events.description: With vasopressor support, fluid, and an adjusted anesthetic, vitals return to an acceptable baseline for the case.

expectedActions.label: Preoxygenate to EtO2 > 80% (3 min or 8 vital-capacity breaths)
Verify IV patency and running fluids before induction
Titrate a reduced propofol dose (about 1-1.5 mg/kg) given age and ACE inhibitor use
expectedActions.description: Verbalizes that the patient's age, hypertension, and morning lisinopril increase hypotension risk and adjusts dosing/plan accordingly.

expectedActions.label: Recognize post-induction hypotension (MAP < 65) and verbalize it
Administer a vasopressor (phenylephrine 100 mcg IV or ephedrine 5-10 mg IV)
expectedActions.description: Chooses phenylephrine if heart rate is adequate, ephedrine if relatively bradycardic; redoses as needed.

expectedActions.label: Give a crystalloid fluid bolus (250-500 mL)
Reduce the volatile agent while hypotensive
Shorten the NIBP cycle (1-2 min) and recheck the pressure
Verbalize a differential for the hypotension
expectedActions.description: Considers drug effect, hypovolemia, anaphylaxis, measurement error, cardiac ischemia, and high intrathoracic pressure.

expectedActions.label: Reassess after each intervention and confirm MAP > 65 before proceeding

expectedProgression: Learners meet the patient, review the chart (noting lisinopril taken today), apply monitors, and preoxygenate.
Standard IV induction produces expected modest blood pressure decline and a surgical depth index (~40).
About 3 minutes in, blood pressure falls to 72/40 with a blunted heart rate response before surgical stimulation.
Learners should recognize the hypotension quickly, shorten the NIBP cycle, and treat with vasopressor boluses plus a fluid bolus.
Reducing sevoflurane and repeating vasopressor doses restores MAP above 65 mmHg.
If untreated, faculty fires the worsening-hypotension event; escalating treatment is then required.
Incision provides sympathetic stimulation, after which hemodynamics stabilize and the case proceeds.
correctManagement: Preoxygenate with a tight mask seal until end-tidal O2 exceeds 80%.
Reduce the propofol induction dose (about 1-1.5 mg/kg) and give it slowly in an elderly hypertensive on an ACE inhibitor.
Recognize hypotension early: verbalize MAP, shorten the NIBP cycle to 1-2 minutes.
Give phenylephrine 100 mcg IV when heart rate is adequate; choose ephedrine 5-10 mg IV if heart rate is low.
Bolus 250-500 mL of crystalloid to address relative hypovolemia after an overnight fast.
Reduce inspired sevoflurane while pressure is low, accepting a transiently lighter depth before incision.
Reassess blood pressure after each intervention and repeat vasopressor doses to keep MAP above 65 mmHg.
Verbalize a differential (drug effect, hypovolemia, anaphylaxis, ischemia, measurement error) and confirm no rash, no bronchospasm, normal ECG.
Communicate with the surgical team about readiness for incision once hemodynamics are acceptable.
commonErrors: Giving a full 2 mg/kg propofol push rapidly without adjusting for age and ACE inhibitor exposure.
Leaving the NIBP cycle at 5 minutes and missing the depth of the pressure drop.
Treating with fluid alone and waiting for it to work while MAP stays below 60 mmHg.
Deepening the anesthetic or giving opioid during hypotension because of a fixed plan for incision.
Repeatedly cycling the cuff without giving any treatment (verification paralysis).
Failing to consider anaphylaxis or other causes when hypotension does not respond as expected.
Not reassessing after the first phenylephrine dose and assuming a single bolus fixed the problem.

points: Post-induction hypotension is common and largely predictable: age, chronic hypertension, ACE inhibitor/ARB continuation, and hypovolemia are the classic risk factors.
ACE inhibitors blunt the renin-angiotensin response to vasodilation, so hypotension is deeper and more refractory; some centers hold them the morning of surgery.
Vasopressor choice: phenylephrine for vasodilation with adequate heart rate; ephedrine when heart rate is also low; small doses, repeated, with reassessment.
Monitoring frequency should match acuity: a 5-minute cuff cycle can hide 4 minutes of profound hypotension.
The volatile agent is itself a vasodilator; temporarily reducing it is a legitimate hemodynamic intervention before stimulation.
MAP below about 65 mmHg is associated with organ injury (myocardial injury, acute kidney injury); duration matters as much as depth.
A brief verbalized differential protects against anchoring on 'it's just the propofol' when the real cause is anaphylaxis or bleeding.
questions: What features of this patient made post-induction hypotension likely, and what would you change about the induction plan next time?
How did you decide between phenylephrine and ephedrine, and what would push you toward the other drug?
At what MAP or duration of hypotension do you become concerned about organ injury, and why?
What monitoring changes did you make (or wish you had made) once the pressure dropped?
What other diagnoses did you consider for hypotension after induction, and what would have made you act on them?
How would your management have changed if the hypotension had not responded to two rounds of vasopressor and fluid?
